State-of-the-art algorithms formalize the multi-model fitting problem.
The multi-model fitting problem involves finding disjoint sets of data points.
The multi-model fitting problem is defined as finding disjoint sets of data points, each representing a model instance.
In some cases, a point belongs to multiple instances, making the traditional point-to-model assignment assumption unsolvable.
The point-to-model assignment can be unclear, especially for points around the intersection of instances.
Recovery of disjoint point sets usually requires a complex procedure like labeling via energy minimization, severely affecting the run-time.
The main contribution of the paper is a new problem formulation that does not require crisp point-to-model assignments but allows a point to be assigned to multiple instances.

The proposed method deviates from the formulations used in state-of-the-art algorithms for multi-model fitting, allowing for simple iterative algorithm yet achieving superior results in accuracy and run-time.

We formulate the problem of selecting different model instances as finding similar ones.
A straightforward strategy for finding similar instances is to find clusters in the consensus space defined over the preference vectors.
In general, this clustering takes place in a large dimensional space, with as many dimensions as the number of input data points.
In this particular setup, we never have more than a few tens of instances to be clustered thanks to the iterative proposal strategy.
The clustering is done on a few high-dimensional vectors that are very efficient with most of the clustering algorithms.
A single model instance rarely has an extreme number of inliers.
The indices of the non-zero elements in v can be stored.
The min hash algorithm can approximately find the inlier overlap in constant time.
The next step after obtaining a set of instance clusters is to replace the instances in each cluster with a single one.
Using density modes, e.g. as in [13], requires doing operations with the preference vectors for density-based clustering.
In the implementation, DBSCAN density-based clustering runs swiftly on the problem and returns accurate solutions.
DBSCAN requires two parameters, i.e., the minimum size cmin of a cluster to be kept and a threshold ϵT to decide if two model instances are neighbors in the consensus space.
The minimum size cmin = 1 since single-element clusters also contain dominant model instances and should be kept.
Setting threshold ϵT to 0 means that models are considered neighbors if and only if their preference vectors are exactly the same.
Models are considered neighbors if and only if their preference vectors are exactly the same.
All methods are neighbors even if they do not share inliers based on the parameter ϵT = 1.
Iteratively re-weighted least-squares approach is applied to improve the parameters of instances kept by the clustering algorithm.
During the optimization step, two instances might become similar and should be put in the same cluster.

Both P-NAPSAC and the proposed Connected Component Sampler exploit the spatial nature of geometric data. P-NAPSAC is randomized and returns minimal samples, while the Connected Component Sampler is deterministic and proposes larger-than-minimal samples.

In this section, the trajectories of objects are estimated.

The trajectory is encoded by the blur kernel, which may not correspond to a physically plausible trajectory. The blur kernel is assumed to be piece-wise linear due to bounces and may contain responses from other moving objects in the scene.
An image of a blurred object is formed as a composition of the blurred object appearance and the background, where the sharp object appearance with mask encodes the object.
Input image and background are assumed to be known in the composition process of the image of a blurred object.
The blur kernel encodes the trajectory of the object in the formation model.
The sharp object appearance with mask encodes the object in the composition of the blurred object image.
Blur kernels contain responses due to moving objects in the scene.
If two fast-moving objects intersect or fly close to each other, the blur kernel will contain multiple responses corresponding to each motion.
Estimated blur kernels are noisy, with many outliers, and contain artifacts.
Blur kernels contain artifacts due to shadows, low contrast, and discretization.
Motion blur priors have been proposed to reduce issues with blur kernels.
Recent methods address the task of extracting final continuous trajectory by employing Sequential RANSAC on thresholded blur kernels.
Sequential RANSAC is employed on the thresholded blur kernels for addressing the task.
Blur kernels are extracted using the TbD method from all sequences in the TbD and TbD-3D datasets.
TbD dataset is simpler since it contains mostly uniformly colored objects moving in the plane parallel to the camera plane.
TbD-3D dataset is more challenging with highly textured objects that are rotating and moving in 3D.
Ground truth sub-frame object location is given from a high-speed camera.
Multiple line segments in each blur kernel are measured by averaging the L2 distance of each ground truth location to the closest fitted line segment.
Implementations provided by state-of-the-art methods are used for the evaluation of average error, standard deviation, and average run-time.
Deviation and average run-time are key metrics used to evaluate state-of-the-art methods. The average run-time is crucial in determining the efficiency of the proposed method.
Deviation has a direct impact on the accuracy of algorithms. A lower deviation often results in higher accuracy.
The performance of algorithms is evaluated based on both accuracy and processing time. A method that outperforms in terms of accuracy may have a longer processing time.
Soft assignment is crucial for finding continuous chains in the data. Without considering soft assignment, the identification of continuous chains becomes challenging.
The presence of continuous chains directly impacts the ability to detect short segments accurately. Failure to identify continuous chains leads to a loss of short segments.

Table 5 provides information on the average accuracy and run-time for multiple line segment detection. These metrics help in analyzing the performance of algorithms in finding trajectories of fast-moving objects.
